title: RA.997 Verraco
tracklist:

translation.content: As part of our countdown to the 1,000th edition of the RA Podcast, a milestone in the 19-year history of Resident Advisor's weekly mix series, we're switching up the usual format. This week, following heady excursions through Lagos, Kampala, Detroit and Chicago, our focus shifts to Latin America—arguably the story in underground electronic music since the pandemic. After years of being all but ignored internationally, the glut of special club sounds coming out of Peru, Brazil, Colombia and beyond have finally received their flowers. Among the movement's great success stories are two artists who exemplify its refreshingly undogmatic energy: Verraco and Bitter Babe. (Check out the latter's mix here.) Verraco handles the A-side. Is there a more compelling electronic artist around right now? Both in the studio and behind the decks, the Medellín maverick currently sits in that coveted creative sweet spot, where every new musical morsel feels like a moment. To bask in any of his singular tracks on VOAM, Timedance and now XL is to be bowled over by their rhythmic brilliance, madcap hooks and whirlpool basslines. ("Basic Maneuvers," anyone?) Verraco couples this talent with a scholarly passion for creating what he calls "sonic exchanges" between the best of Latin American dance music and global hubs like Berlin and Bristol. (His debut podcast, in 2019, went big on that tip, blending Arca with Mika Vainio.) Many of these hybrid experiments have found their way onto TraTraTrax, the label he co-runs with Nyksan and DJ Lomalinda that's become a beacon for the Latin scene. Its rise, he writes below, is "the pride of my life." Like with a lot of what Verraco does, RA.997 is a slippery one to describe—so why not let the man himself do the honours: "an intersection between dub-infused techno but with some flow, reduced atmospheric tribal, edgy bass cuts, mental emo-tek?" True to form, plenty of unreleased TraTraTrax bits rub shoulders with Virginia, A Made Up Sound and a euphoric edit of Ploy's "Ramos." Pure class, in other words. -Carlos Hawthorn How and where was the mix recorded? And can you tell us the idea behind it? At my girlfriend's house, in her booth, but with four rented CDJs and my mixer, a new boosted version of the Allen & Heath 92. Sonically, I insist on recording mixes in a similar way—intention, layers, intensity or technique—as I do when I play in a club, but this time trying to also fulfil the purpose of home-listening. So it's an intersection between dub-infused techno but with some flow, reduced atmospheric tribal, edgy bass cuts, mental emo-tek? And several new edits and remixes that I made to test in the summer. What have you been up to recently? Enjoying being in Lisbon with people that I love for a couple of weeks while going through this summer tour in Europe. How has TraTraTrax shaped your career? I don't feel that TraTraTrax has shaped my career. For personal reasons I've decided to take a different path that depends on my productions and sound design, on my techno and bass backgrounds, on my DJing, and not only on the success of a label. However, it's perhaps the pride of my life to have been able to create a platform together with my colleagues and work on it every day and give amplification to several South American friends and artists—to the point of even changing their lives. A platform that contributed enormously to the fact that electronic music made in South America stopped being criminally ignored. A platform that no longer looks upwards in an aspirational way and, on the contrary, is dictating and influencing what gets played on some dance floors around the world. What would you love the rest of the world to know or understand about Latin electronic culture that they don't already? To call or say "Latin club" to all the new electronic music coming out of Latin America is as reductive and ignorant as saying "world music" to the music coming from outside the western axis. What do you hope to accomplish (musically or otherwise) before the end of 2025? Buy furniture for my mostly empty flat in Bogotá.
translation.blurb: The Medellín maverick opens RA.997 with another mind-altering home run.
